data.title: 4 Dividend Stocks that Could Be Your Life Jacket In This Manic Market
data.description:
Dividend stocks are having a moment, and it's not hard to see why. The S&P 500 may have staged a 9.5% comeback after Trump hit pause on most of his global tariffs, but with a potential recession looming, investors are still feeling jumpy. And when the outlook gets murky, dividends start to look a lot like life jackets.

Dividend Aristocrats Offer Shelter in a Storm

Companies like Coca-Cola, Colgate-Palmolive, Consolidated Edison, and Caterpillar — known as Dividend Aristocrats for consistently raising their payouts for 25+ years — are winning fresh attention. The ProShares S&P 500 Dividend Aristocrats ETF (NOBL) is down just 15% from its peak, while the broader S&P 500 is off 17%. That spread may not sound huge, but in a storm, every inch of shelter matters. During past market meltdowns, these names typically fell far less than the average stock and recovered faster.

These Stocks Could Be Near the Bottom

Even better? Many high-quality dividend names have already sold off significantly, suggesting they may be closer to a bottom than other parts of the market. In five major downturns since 2000, the average Dividend Aristocrat fell 25% peak-to-trough, versus 40% for the S&P 500. And with tariff tensions unresolved — especially with China still facing a 125% levy — the flight to safety may continue.

1. Coca-Cola (KO)

Coca-Cola (KO) has raised dividends for 62 straight years and yields just under 3.2%. Its iconic brand and global footprint make it a classic defensive pick. Even with tariffs weighing on shipping and ingredient costs, analysts say its pricing power and steady demand keep it resilient. According to Morningstar, Coke's wide moat and brand equity help it weather almost any economic shock.

2. Colgate-Palmolive (CL)

Colgate-Palmolive (CL) hasn't missed a dividend hike in 60 years. The household staples giant yields about 2.6%, and demand for its products tends to stay stable even when wallets tighten. Analysts at Goldman Sachs noted Colgate's global diversification and lean supply chain give it an edge as trade tensions drag on.

3. Consolidated Edison (ED)

Utility stocks like Consolidated Edison (ED) often shine when markets wobble. With a 3.5% dividend yield and a 49-year streak of increases, it's one of the more reliable names in the sector. While higher interest rates can weigh on utilities, Ed's regulated structure and stable customer base help balance the risk. According to CFRA Research, it offers downside protection many investors crave right now.

4. Caterpillar (CAT)

Caterpillar (CAT) may not seem like a safe haven at first glance. But with 30 years of dividend growth and an industrial edge, it's proving surprisingly durable. Shares surged nearly 10% this week, partly on hopes that infrastructure spending and domestic demand can offset export headwinds. CAT's exposure to the U.S. construction market makes it a compelling pick even in shaky times.

Stagflation Fears Boost the Case for Dividends

The real fear now is stagflation. Think high inflation plus low growth — a nasty combo last seen in the 1970s. Barclays strategist Venu Krishna told Barron's he sees scenarios where 2025 earnings growth could be wiped out completely. And while the 10-year Treasury yield holds firm, that's not much comfort to stockholders staring at flat profits. Dividend payouts, however modest, can soften the blow. Aristocrats yield around 2.8% and pay out roughly 65% of free cash flow, meaning these aren't reckless spenders but steady growers.

Dividend stocks won't dazzle with overnight gains. But they don't have to. Right now, staying afloat might be the smartest move of all. You can compare these dividend stocks using the TipRanks Stocks Comparison tool to see analyst ratings, dividend yields, and access many more investor-friendly features.

Investors can view upcoming dividend payout dates using TipRanks’ Dividend Calendar.

data.author: Annika Masrani

data.title: Tech in Turmoil: How Tariffs, Trade Wars, and AI Rivalries Are Reshaping the Industry in 2025
data.description:
The tech industry in 2025 isn't just riding the wave of innovation – it's navigating a stormy sea of tariffs, geopolitical conflicts, and relentless competition. Imagine trying to code your next big product launch while dodging trade bullets, wrestling with AI ethics, and keeping one ear on the news in case another deus ex machina ruins your plans and strategy. Welcome to the new normal.

In this piece, we'll break down the challenges the tech sector has been facing since the beginning of 2025 and, subsequently, Donald Trump's second term. We’ll discuss tariffs, Geopolitics, competition, and economic reality.

Tariffs: The Price of Playing Global

When global trade thought it could take a breather, tariffs returned. The U.S.–China standoff has triggered fresh import duties, hitting everything from microchips to high-end servers. Tech companies that depend on global supply chains, like NVIDIA (NVDA) and Apple (AAPL), feel the burn as semiconductor prices heat up. Higher costs for semiconductors and rare components are squeezing profit margins and forcing companies to rethink where (and how) they make their stuff.

In the latest twist, President Trump has announced a 90-day postponement on new tariffs, but only for countries outside China. It's a small sigh of relief for companies but with a nervous side-eye at what happens on Day 91.

This patchy approach creates a game of geopolitical hopscotch. Fragile supply chains are being rerouted through Vietnam, India, and Mexico post-pandemic. That sounds smart until you hit unexpected speed bumps like unstable infrastructure or labor shortages. For instance, Intel (INTC) is doubling down on U.S. and European fabs, but construction delays and talent gaps are slowing progress.

Geopolitics: The Global Chessboard

Now, toss in the high-stakes chess match of global politics. The U.S.–China rivalry is still on, while Russia's moves in Ukraine are still sending shockwaves through Europe's energy and tech dependencies. Countries are racing to secure semiconductor fabs like they're modern-day gold mines.

Governments are offering enticing subsidies to lure chipmakers. Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM) is building plants in Arizona, Samsung (SMSN) is pouring billions into Texas, and ASML (ASML) is navigating EU-U.S. alliances like a seasoned diplomat.

However, all this fab-building means nothing without skilled workers. The chip industry is facing a global talent drought, especially for engineers trained in cutting-edge manufacturing and AI architecture. Companies can print money, but they can't print expertise.

Competition: It's a Modern-Day Arms Race

Fast, furious, and unforgiving competition is at the heart of it all. Everyone wants a piece of the next big thing, whether it's generative AI, 6G, or quantum computing. Microsoft (MSFT) is racing to integrate AI into its cloud products, Alphabet (GOOGL) is doubling down on cybersecurity, and Palantir (PLTR) is pushing military-grade data analytics into civilian markets.

But innovation doesn't come cheap or easy. Regulators worldwide are tightening regulations on privacy, data use, and environmental impact. Europe's Digital Services Act is in full swing, and even the U.S. is flirting with more aggressive antitrust rules.

This means companies aren't just building new tech and legal teams and compliance departments faster than you can say "algorithmic bias." Staying ahead requires more than coding skills. It demands diplomacy and foresight, and some might suggest a crystal ball.

The Economic Reality

All these forces – tariffs, politics, and innovation – reshape the tech world’s economics. According to some forecasts, global IT spending is still growing, projecting 8.3%- 9.8% in 2025, but inflation and interest rates keep CFOs cautious. Big spenders like Amazon (AMZN) and Meta (META) are dialing back capex plans, prioritizing efficiency and profitability over moonshots.

That doesn't mean the opportunity is dead. It just means it's hiding in less obvious places. Think green computing, secure supply chain software, and regional AI models tailored for local markets.

A Delicate Balancing Act

So where does that leave us? In a word, unsettled.

We're very much in a volatile moment. Tariffs could rise or fall on a presidential whim, and competition is heating up. Even giants like Tesla (TSLA) and IBM (IBM) are learning that dominance today doesn't guarantee relevance tomorrow. The most competent companies (and investors) are hedging their bets: diversifying supply chains, staying agile with R&D, and keeping a close eye on policy and public opinion. Because in 2025, the real advantage isn't size. It's adaptability.

The Bottom Line

This isn't just a storm; we're witnessing a full-blown climate shift. Tariffs, geopolitics, and competition are reshaping the global tech industry in real time. While there's no playbook for this kind of turbulence, those who can adapt quickly and spot opportunities in chaos will come out ahead.

data.author: Ran Melamed

data.title: JPMorgan Chase & Co. (JPM) Is About to Report Its Q4 Earnings Tomorrow. Here Is What to Expect
data.description:
U.S. banking giant JPMorgan Chase (JPM) is set to release its fourth quarter 2025 financials on April 11. JPM stock has declined about 1% year-to-date, primarily due to slower loan growth, rising expenses from investments in technology, and increasing regulatory and political scrutiny on big banks. Wall Street analysts expect the company to report earnings per share of $4.63, representing a 4.3% increase year-over-year.

Meanwhile, revenues are expected to decrease by 34% from the year-ago quarter to $43.99 billion, according to data from the TipRanks Forecast page.

Analyst’s Views Ahead of JPM's Q1 Earnings

Ahead of JPMorgan's Q1earnings, Piper Sandler, R. Scott Siefers reduced his price target from $275 to $270 but maintained a Buy rating on the stock. He believes that JPMorgan is well-positioned to do well in the current uncertain market. The analyst points out JPMorgan’s large Markets business, which he believes will help counter weakness in the Investment Banking sector and support earnings.

Moreover, Siefers believes JPMorgan's strong credit, liquidity, and capital position will help it keep its momentum and stay flexible in managing growth.

According to Main Street Data, JPM's Commercial and Investment Bank segment brought in $17.6 billion in revenue in Q4. Despite weak consumer spending, it will be worth watching if this segment continues to show strength in the coming quarters.

Options Traders Anticipate a Minor Move

Using TipRanks' Options tool, we can see what options traders are expecting from the stock immediately after its earnings report. The expected earnings move is determined by calculating the at-the-money straddle of the options closest to expiration after the earnings announcement. If this sounds complicated, don't worry, the Options tool does this for you.

Indeed, it currently says that options traders are expecting a 4.92% move in either direction.

What Is the Price Target for JPM?

Turning to Wall Street, JPM stock has a Moderate Buy consensus rating based on 11 Buys and seven Holds assigned in the last three months. At $270.31, the average JPMorgan price target implies a 15.35% upside potential.

data.title: Anthropic Fires Up Claude Max Plan for Users Who Never Log Off
data.description:
Anthropic just dropped its Claude's “Max” plan—a high-powered tier built for the AI faithful who hit usage caps like clockwork. The new offering sits above the existing $20/month Claude Pro, with options priced at $100 and $200 per month depending on how much firepower you need.

Anthropic Targets Heavy-Duty Professionals

If you’ve been stretching Claude to its limits, you’re not alone. According to product lead Scott White, Anthropic created Max after seeing a "large number of users integrating Claude deeply into their workflows," only to run headfirst into usage limits. The $100 plan offers 5x the current Pro cap. The $200 tier? That one's for the diehards—20x the limits and early access to new tools.

White explained the rationale clearly: "We want to give people choice on the type of plan they can use to get the scale of usage that they want." Think of Max as the bridge between casual AI use and enterprise-grade muscle.

A Shot Across OpenAI's Bow

This isn't just about bandwidth. It's about Anthropic staking its claim in the competitive AI subscription wars. OpenAI's $200/month Pro plan for ChatGPT has long catered to developers and researchers. Now Claude has its answer. And while it doesn't offer API-level access just yet, Max subscribers will be first in line for experimental features and upgrades.

It's a subtle but clear escalation in the fight for premium AI customers—especially those building tools, writing code, or running research at scale.

Why Max Might Matter More Than It Seems

Beyond pricing tiers and limits, this move signals Anthropic's confidence in Claude's staying power. The company's Claude 3 models have been praised for their reasoning skills and long-context abilities. Giving power users more runway to work could deepen Claude's niche among developers and professionals looking for more than just casual chat.

Max may not be for everyone, but for those who've made Claude their go-to assistant, this plan's about keeping up with their ambition—without throttling their flow.

What Is the Best AI Stock to Buy?

data.title: Stock Market News Today, 4/10/25 – Futures Down after Trump's Tariff Delay Sparks Historic Rally
data.description:
U.S. stock futures dipped on Thursday morning, taking a breather after the major indices witnessed a historic rally in the previous session. The rally was fueled by President Donald Trump’s unexpected 90-day pause on many of his newly imposed tariffs. Futures on the Nasdaq 100 (NDX), the Dow Jones Industrial Average (DJIA), and the S&P 500 (SPX) were up 1.86%, 1.18%, and 1.53%, respectively, at 4:18 a.m. EST, April 10.

In Wednesday’s regular trading session, the Dow Jones and the S&P 500 soared by 7.87% and 9.52%, respectively, marking their largest single-day percentage increases in five years. The Nasdaq Composite (NDAQ) also saw a remarkable 12.16% jump, its best single-day performance since 2001.

The rally was heavily led by megacap tech stocks, with strong performances from Nvidia (NVDA) up 18.7%, Tesla (TSLA) gained 22.7%, Apple (AAPL) jumped 15.3%, Meta (META) up 14.8%, and Amazon (AMZN) rose 12%.

Importantly, yesterday's trading also saw historically high volume, with around 30 billion shares traded, the highest level in at least 18 years.

The market’s enthusiasm was aided by Trump’s decision to grant a 90-day reprieve on reciprocal tariffs for countries that had not retaliated against previous U.S. tariffs, while the 10% baseline tariff remains in place.

However, the trade war with China continued to escalate, with Trump raising tariffs on Chinese goods to 125%. Moreover, measures against the European Union are likely to proceed due to Brussels’ earlier retaliatory comments.

Looking ahead, investors are awaiting key economic data releases, such as the March Consumer Price Index (CPI) and Initial Jobless Claims for the week ended April 5. Also, several Federal Reserve officials are slated to speak today, which will provide insights into the central bank's future monetary policy.

On the earnings front, CarMax (KMX) and Lovesac (LOVE) are scheduled to report their quarterly numbers today.

Meanwhile, the U.S. 10-year treasury yield was down, floating near 4.289%. Simultaneously, WTI crude oil futures are trending lower, hovering near $60.71 per barrel as of the last check.

Elsewhere, European indices opened in the green today, buoyed by President Trump’s announcement of a temporary 90-day pause on new tariffs for over 75 trading partners.

Asia-Pacific Markets Rallied on Thursday

Asia-Pacific indices soared today. The rally came after Trump lowered new tariff rates on imports from most U.S. trade partners to 10% for a 90-day period to facilitate trade negotiations.

At the same time, Hong Kong's Hang Seng Index was up 2.27%. Further, China's Shanghai Composite and the Shenzhen Component indices gained 1.16% and 2.25%, respectively. Also, Japan's Nikkei and Topix indices closed higher by 9.13% and 8.09%, respectively.

Interested in more economic insights? Tune in to our LIVE webinar.

data.author: Radhika Saraogi

data.title: Magnificent 7 Stocks Add Over $1.5T in Market Cap After Trump Pauses Tariffs
data.description:
Wall Street's "Magnificent 7" stocks added more than $1.5 trillion in market capitalization yesterday, after President Donald Trump put a pause on reciprocal tariffs for 90 days. Electric vehicle (EV) maker Tesla (TSLA) led the gains with a 22.7% surge, while chipmaker Nvidia (NVDA) jumped 18.7%. Surprisingly, iPhone-maker Apple (AAPL) rallied 15.3% even though Trump did not pause the 125% tariffs on Chinese imports. Following the announcement, Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said, "Do not retaliate, and you will be rewarded," as if signaling China to stop retaliating with countermeasures.

It was a strong day for global stock markets, after days of bleeding trillions of dollars in value. Stocks were already climbing higher during intra-day trading in anticipation of trade negotiations. The White House announced a 90-day pause on all reciprocal tariffs announced on "Liberation Day," pushing all major indices higher. The S&P 500 index (SPX) gained 9.52%, the Dow Jones Industrial Average (DJIA) notched up 7.87%, and the tech-heavy Nasdaq ended up 12.16%.

Tariffs Pause Gives a Breather to Rethink Investment Strategy

The break on potential tariff concerns gives a breather to the managements to rethink their investment strategy. Until their Q1 Fiscal 2025 results, major tech companies were beaming with pride over their billion-dollar bets on artificial intelligence (AI) and data center infrastructure. However, Trump's tariffs prompted them to reconsider their massive investments, since they levied heavy taxes on the hardware coming from Taiwan, South Korea, and China.

Despite the temporary respite, there is still ambiguity on how the final tariffs from different trading nations will take shape, post negotiations with the White House. The earnings season for the second quarter is just around the corner, and investors will closely monitor how big tech companies adjust their investment and capex budgets in response to the taxes. Meanwhile, Google parent Alphabet (GOOGL) was the first big tech company to confirm that it will stick to its planned $75 billion spending for Fiscal 2025.

Is it Time to Buy the Magnificent 7 Stocks?

Despite the surge in the Magnificent 7 stocks yesterday, their valuation is a far cry from the peaks of 2024. Investors can consider investing in one of them after thorough research.

We used the TipRanks' Stock Comparison Tool for Magnificent 7 Stocks to see which one is most favored by analysts.

Currently, Nvidia, Microsoft (MSFT), Amazon (AMZN), and Meta Platforms (META) score Wall Street's "Strong Buy" consensus rating, with NVDA stock offering the highest upside potential among them.

data.author: Sheryl Sheth

data.title: U.S. Markets Surge as Trump's Tariff Pause Triggers Historic Rally
data.description:
On April 9, 2025, U.S. stock markets erupted in a historic rally, with the Nasdaq delivering its most explosive performance since 2001. The rally came after President Donald Trump unexpectedly announced a 90-day suspension of planned tariff hikes on imports from most countries, with the notable exception of China, which will now face a steep 125% tariff on U.S.-bound goods.

The move, seen as a temporary easing of global trade tensions, sparked a massive rebound across equities. The Nasdaq 100 (QQQ) surged 12%, while the S&P 500 (SPY) jumped 9%, its biggest gain since 2008. The Dow Jones Industrial Average (DJIA) climbed more than 3,000 points, closing up nearly 9% as investor sentiment flipped overnight.

Tech Stocks Lead the Charge

Technology giants, especially those battered in recent sell-offs, led the explosive gains. Apple (AAPL) surged 11%, regaining over $260 billion in market capitalization. Microsoft (MSFT) jumped 10.5%, while Nvidia (NVDA) spiked 12.3%, riding investor optimism around AI demand and supply chain relief.

Meanwhile, Amazon (AMZN) and Meta (META) each advanced more than 10%, contributing to a stunning recovery for the tech sector. Altogether, the “Magnificent Seven” tech companies added nearly over $1.8 trillion in market cap, erasing much of the losses from previous weeks.

Wall Street Cheers as Tariff Pause Boosts Market Confidence

Trump’s tariff pause not only boosted stocks but also sparked a rally throughout the entire market. The WSJ Dollar Index briefly popped after the news but still ended the day slightly lower, showing some investors remain cautious. Gold had its best day since 2023, jumping to around $3,100 a troy ounce, as some traders stayed defensive. At the same time, U.S. crude futures surged over 4%, closing at $62.35 a barrel, helped by renewed hopes for global demand.

The economic outlook also got an upgrade. Goldman Sachs scrapped its earlier recession forecast, saying the delay in tariffs could help keep growth on track, at least for now. Meanwhile, Japan's top currency official promised to work with global partners to keep financial markets stable.

Which Magnificent Seven Stocks Offer the Most Upside?

Using the TipRanks' Stock Comparison Tool for Magnificent 7 Stocks, analysts see the greatest upside in NVDA stock forecast, projecting a potential gain of almost 53%. Amazon follows with a forecasted 38% upside. Despite a neutral outlook on Tesla, analysts still expect 13% growth, largely driven by the stock's recent slump.

Currently, Nvidia, Microsoft (MSFT) Amazon (AMZN) and Meta Platforms (META) score Wall Street's "Strong Buy" consensus rating, with NVDA stock offering the highest upside potential.

data.author: Shalu Saraf

data.title: "Do Not Retaliate, and You Will Be Rewarded," Says U.S. Treasury Secretary Bessent
data.description:
U.S. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent spoke briefly after President Donald Trump announced a 90-day pause on reciprocal tariffs for over 75 countries while also increasing tariffs on Chinese imports. More specifically, Bessent said, “Do not retaliate, and you will be rewarded,” which sends a clear signal to countries that cooperation with the U.S. could lead to better trade terms. This also caused markets to stage a sharp reversal to the upside.

Unsurprisingly, Bessent praised Trump's trade strategy by saying that the President's tough approach encouraged more than 75 nations to come to the negotiating table. He also called the decision to escalate tariffs on China a result of its refusal to cooperate. It is worth noting that tariffs on Chinese goods were raised to 125%, up from earlier levels.

Earlier in the day, Bessent criticized China's retaliatory tariffs by calling them a losing move. He said it was disappointing that China was not willing to negotiate and labeled the country "the worst offenders in the international trading system." His comments highlight the administration's belief that their aggressive stance is pushing most nations to engage while isolating China for its lack of cooperation.

Is SPY a Buy Right Now?

Turning to Wall Street, analysts have a Moderate Buy consensus rating on the SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY) based on 409 Buys, 87 Holds, and eight Sells assigned in the past three months, as indicated by the graphic below. Furthermore, the average SPY price target of $688.17 per share implies 26.8% upside potential.

data.title: BTC, ETH, DOGE: Crypto Prices Surge on News of Tariff Relief
data.description:
Cryptocurrencies are rallying higher alongside stocks on news that U.S. President Donald Trump is easing up on his tariff policies.

Bitcoin (BTC), the largest cryptocurrency by market capitalization, is up 7% in afternoon trading on April 9 after President Trump announced that he is pausing for 90 days U.S. import duties on most countries and moving to adopt a universal duty of 10% for most foreign products. BTC is now trading at $82,500. It had been as low as $77,000 in recent days.

Other digital coins and tokens are also surging higher on news of the tariff relief, with Ethereum (ETH) up 13%, Solana (SOL) up 15%, and Dogecoin (DOGE) rising 12%. Stocks associated with crypto such as MicroStrategy (MSTR), Robinhood Markets (HOOD), and Coinbase (COIN) are also skyrocketing, with each up more than 20% on the day.

Risk Assets Rebound

Cryptocurrencies and stocks are rebounding strongly as some of the uncertainty related to Trump’s tariff policies abates and investors flood back into risk assets. However, crypto prices had fallen steeply in recent weeks, with Bitcoin dropping 25% from its January all-time high. Over the past few days, crypto had largely traded in-tandem with equities, both of which had fallen sharply lower.

Bitcoin and other digital assets are now rising with stocks in the biggest rally in five years for the blue-chip Dow Jones Industrial Average. However, the trade volatility may not be over as President Trump also announced that he is raising tariffs on Chinese imports to 125%, the highest level yet. Chinese officials in Beijing have vowed to fight back against U.S. trade protectionism.

Is BTC a Buy?

Most Wall Street firms don't offer ratings or price targets on Bitcoin, so we'll look at the cryptocurrency's three-month performance instead. As one can see in the chart below, the price of BTC has declined 19.10% in the last 12 weeks.

data.title: Trump Drops Tariffs to 10% 'Universal Rate' for Most Countries, Hits China with 125% Duty
data.description:
Details are emerging on U.S. President Donald Trump’s 90-day tariff pause that has stocks staging their biggest one-day rally in five years.

On social media, Trump said that he is pausing for 90 days the full effect of his reciprocal tariffs on most countries and moving to implement a lower import duty of 10% on most foreign products entering the U.S., which the president called a “universal rate.”

However, rather than lower the tariffs on China, America’s largest trading partner, Trump doubled down, announcing that he is raising the tariffs imposed on imports from China to 125% "effective immediately." On social media, Trump wrote: “Based on the lack of respect that China has shown to the World's Markets, I am hereby raising the Tariff charged to China by the United States of America to 125%, effective immediately.”

Trade War?

Also on social media, President Trump said he was pausing and lowering tariff rates because “more than 75 countries” have contacted U.S. officials “to negotiate a solution” to trade concerns that he raised in imposing the new import duties. U.S. Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick said on social media that “the world is ready to work with President Trump to fix global trade.”

The de-escalation of Trump’s tariffs is being cheered by investors and sending all three of the major U.S. indices skyrocketing, with the Nasdaq Composite index up 10% in afternoon trading on April 9. However, the higher duties imposed on China raises the chances of a major trade war between the world’s two biggest economies, a development that could tip the global economy into recession, say analysts.

Is the Vanguard S&P 500 ETF a Buy?

The Vanguard S&P 500 ETF (VOO) which tracks the movements of the benchmark U.S. stock index, has a consensus Moderate Buy rating among 506 Wall Street analysts. That rating is based on 409 Buy, 89 Hold, and eight Sell recommendations made in the last three months. The average VOO price target of $639.90 implies 31.59% upside from current levels.
